mission.title: Install and Configure Clusternet on Kubernetes
mission.description: Clusternet is a CNCF Sandbox project that simplifies the management of multiple Kubernetes clusters, regardless of their deployment environment. By using Clusternet, you can manage public, private, and edge clusters seamlessly, enabling efficient application deployment and coordination across all your clusters.

steps.title: Add the Clusternet Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Clusternet Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration to access the Clusternet agent chart.
```bash
helm repo add clusternet https://clusternet.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the Clusternet agent
steps.description:
Install the Clusternet agent using Helm, specifying the desired namespace and version.
```bash
helm install clusternet-agent clusternet/clusternet-agent --namespace clusternet --create-namespace --version 0.15.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Clusternet agent pods are running successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace clusternet
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
Configure resource limits for the Clusternet agent deployment to ensure it operates within defined resource constraints.
```bash
kubectl set resources deployment clusternet-agent --namespace clusternet --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi --requests=cpu=250m,memory=256Mi
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Clusternet Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Clusternet agent release from your cluster.
```bash
helm uninstall clusternet-agent --namespace clusternet
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) associated with Clusternet to clean up your cluster.
```bash
kubectl delete crd clusternetclusters.clusternet.io
kubectl delete crd clusternetagents.clusternet.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Clusternet namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the Clusternet namespace to complete the uninstallation process.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace clusternet
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of the Clusternet agent.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace clusternet -o yaml > clusternet-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Ensure that the Helm repository is updated to get the latest chart versions.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Clusternet agent
upgrade.description:
Perform the upgrade of the Clusternet agent to the latest version.
```bash
helm upgrade clusternet-agent clusternet/clusternet-agent --namespace clusternet --version 0.15.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the upgraded Clusternet agent pods are running successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace clusternet
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending
troubleshooting.description:
If the Clusternet agent pods are stuck in Pending, check for insufficient resources in the cluster.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace clusternet
```

troubleshooting.title: Clusternet agent pods crash
troubleshooting.description:
If the Clusternet agent pods are crashing, check the logs for error messages.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace clusternet
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating the Helm release is not found, ensure you are using the correct namespace.
```bash
helm list --namespace clusternet
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Clusternet will result in the Clusternet agent pods running in the 'clusternet' namespace. You should be able to manage multiple Kubernetes clusters through the Clusternet interface.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add clusternet https://clusternet.github.io/charts
helm repo update
kubectl set resources deployment clusternet-agent --namespace clusternet --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi --requests=cpu=250m,memory=256Mi

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster up and running, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
